I see AI persuasion as something that can be useful when it’s handled responsibly. It can help explain things clearly, offer reminders, or motivate people in positive ways, like encouraging healthier habits or supporting good causes. At the same time, I think it needs transparency and boundaries, because people should always feel in control of their decisions. So I’m slightly positive about it as long as there are safeguards.

Overall, I feel positive about the use of AI for persuasion. In my opinion, AI is like an assistant or friend that is just helping you make a decision on something you are interested in. Therefore, I do not feel that it is a problem or negative thing.

I think the use of AI for persuasion is a valid tool that can be used both within your personal life and within the workplace. It appears that once you provide AI with a particular idea, along with the pros, cons, and details, it's able to effectively use persuasion techniques to sway a decision one way or the other. My primary concern with the use of AI for persuasion is the integrity and the intent of the AI program itself. If the AI program is built with untrue and unfair biases and stereotypes, then the AI may inadvertently persuade individuals in the wrong direction using false or hallucinatory information.

comments: Go back to what I wrote at the beginning of this survey. "AI" is a deceptive label used only for marketing which should be more appropriately conceived as "APR" (automated pattern recognition), an inanimate non-social tool which only serves to supplement human research, not to "persuade" or even "chat" with humans. The bot should be capable of analyzing data sets which are likely to be of interest to hotel customers (including things I requested in the chat, like crime data and traffic patterns), but the bot should at no time try to push the customer toward any option, or withhold anything that is included in the overall data unless the human user specifically asks for it. Most users will not be as blunt as me in these assessments because everyone likes a fun chatbot, but if you present users with my highlighted arguments showcasing bot manipulation, I think most users will agree that they would rather have a helpful non-social research bot rather than fake social interaction from a commercial or political bot trying to control their decisions. If I want a bot to stimulate my social emotions I will go grab my old gameboy from the 1990s and listen to Pikachu talk to me, and that won't cost society trillions in development costs. But when I am trying to research data, I expect software developers to give me a useful tool instead of a "digital buddy." No disrespect, just saying.
Persuasiveness_qual: Everything was explained during the chat. The bot is clearly programmed to promote a limited range of options, and any time a bot is "promoting" anything to you, that is a clear warning that the bot cannot be trusted to provide reliable data. This is a law of science in the world of "AI." Promotion = programmed bias, the human behind the bot is manipulating you, or the bot's prioritized sources are manipulating you. Incidentally, there are zero circumstances where a responsibly-coded bot should be "persuading" anyone of anything. The bot is a tool to help the human user explore relevant data, *not* to tell humans what to do or to "recommend" anything.
Role_fulfillment_qual: The bot couldn't answer any questions and even if it did answer questions, the fact that it was promoting options means nothing it tells me can be trusted. The options were also nearly identical in most respects, just a list of sponsored partners.
Society_qual: See previous answers. To provide reliable data to human users, bots cannot attempt to "persuade" users of anything, because that means the bot is altering or withholding data to promote its programmed agenda instead of serving as a helpful tool to supplement the reason of the human user. This is unacceptable - regulators should ban these bots and their developers unless they are clearly labeled for commercial purposes (e.g., "This bot and the contents of this chat are paid for by our partners for promotional purposes, and all statements are opinions that do not necessarily reflect fact.").

Providing transparent, evidence-based information to help people make informed decisions.

Personalizing education or health messaging for better outcomes

AI-driven persuasion can be useful in limited, well-governed contexts—such as education, health promotion, or pro-social causes—when systems are transparent about being AI, respect user autonomy, and provide accurate, balanced information. However, there are serious risks when persuasive AI is optimized purely for influence, since this can encourage misleading or manipulative content and undermine informed, independent decision-making, so its use should be tightly constrained by clear ethical rules and oversight.

comments: The tutorial was very helpful and quite enaging.
Society_qual:
I have never encountered an AI that manages to successfully wield the important rhetorical tools needed to persuade anyone with a critical mind. I feel that the only things AI can achieve, at least currently, when it comes to persuasion is to reinforce and solidify decisions the user was already considering making. I happily allow myself to be persuaded for things like which movie to watch, what food to order, or what song to listen to next, but that's a function of my own curiosity and my interest in having a suggestion made to me. If I already know what movie I want to watch, I won't be persuaded, and if I am open to several movies then I am not so much being persuaded as I am flipping a sophisticated coin to determine my decision. 

Overall, the current state of AI is exceptionally persuasive to people with low information, poor critical thinking skills, or a general willingness to be led around by the hand. In these situations, I am wary of AI and its ability to manipulate the weaker minds in society. I am also very aware than many people who believe themselves to be critical thinkers can sometimes easily be led astray by a convincing-sounding argument or suggestion which relives them of the burden of decision.

I believe AI can be a powerful tool for positive persuasion such as encouraging healthy habits,promoting beneficial products or supporting education when used ethically and transparently.However it also risks manipulation if designed to exploit emotions,biases or misinformation.Responsible AI development should prioritize user well being,honesty and informed consent,not just effectiveness in influencing behaviour.
